questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Which memory system has an essentially unlimited capacity and stores information relatively permanently?

choices.text: Procedural memory
choices.explanation: Procedural memory is a type of long-term memory specifically for motor skills — though durable, it is a subset of long-term memory, not long-term memory itself.

choices.text: Working memory
choices.explanation: Working memory (short-term memory) is severely limited in both capacity (~7 ± 2 chunks) and duration (seconds to a minute without rehearsal).

choices.text: Iconic memory
choices.explanation: Iconic memory is a form of sensory memory that stores visual images for less than a second — it is temporary and is a precursor to working memory, not long-term storage.

choices.text: Long-term memory
choices.explanation: Long-term memory is the relatively permanent and unlimited storage system that can hold information for minutes to a lifetime — unlike working memory's severe capacity limit.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: In a study of eyewitness memory, participants viewed a film of a car accident. Afterward, half were asked, 'About how fast were the cars going when they smashed into each other?' The other half were asked, 'About how fast were the cars going when they hit each other?' The 'smashed' group gave significantly higher speed estimates and were more likely to report seeing broken glass (which was not present in the film) one week later.

questions.question: Which psychologist's research does this study most directly replicate, and what does it demonstrate?

choices.text: B.F. Skinner; operant conditioning — participants were reinforced for giving higher speed estimates
choices.explanation: Operant conditioning involves learning through reinforcement and punishment — no reinforcement was given in this study; the effect is a memory distortion phenomenon, not behavioral conditioning.

choices.text: Elizabeth Loftus; the misinformation effect — post-event information can alter existing memories
choices.explanation: Loftus's classic experiments showed that the wording of questions (implying greater impact with 'smashed') can introduce false information into memory — participants 'remembered' broken glass that was never in the film, demonstrating how memories are reconstructive, not photographic.

choices.text: Wolfgang Kohler; insight learning — participants suddenly 'remembered' seeing broken glass when cued
choices.explanation: Kohler studied insight problem-solving in chimpanzees — the sudden false memory here results from memory reconstruction, not insight; this is Loftus's misinformation effect.

choices.text: George Miller; chunking — participants encode speed information more efficiently when given leading cues
choices.explanation: Miller's work addressed short-term memory capacity (7 ± 2 chunks) — this study involves long-term memory distortion through misleading post-event information, not encoding efficiency.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: A student studying for a psychology exam reads each definition once (structural encoding), says each term aloud and notes the sound of it (phonemic encoding), or writes a sentence using each term in a relevant context (semantic encoding). When tested a week later, the students using semantic encoding recall significantly more terms.

questions.question: These results most directly support which theory of memory?

choices.text: Multi-store model — information must pass through each store sequentially; semantic encoding improves transfer from STM to LTM
choices.explanation: The multi-store model describes memory as three sequential stores — while related, it doesn't directly explain why meaning-based processing is superior; that explanation belongs specifically to the levels of processing model.

choices.text: Levels of processing theory — deeper, meaning-based encoding produces stronger and more durable memory traces
choices.explanation: The levels of processing model (Craik and Lockhart) proposes that the depth of encoding determines memory durability — semantic encoding (meaning-based) is the deepest level and produces the strongest memory traces, explaining the superiority of the contextual sentence group.

choices.text: Encoding specificity — semantic encoding creates context cues that match the test environment
choices.explanation: Encoding specificity predicts that recall is better when retrieval conditions match encoding conditions — while related, the question specifically tests meaning-based processing superiority, which is most directly predicted by levels of processing theory.

choices.text: Working memory model — semantic encoding uses the phonological loop more efficiently, preserving recall
choices.explanation: The phonological loop handles verbal/acoustic information — it is the phonemic (not semantic) condition that most directly involves the phonological loop; the semantic advantage is explained by depth of processing, not phonological loop efficiency.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: In a free recall experiment, participants study a list of 30 words and immediately recall as many as possible. The serial position curve below describes the pattern: words at the beginning and end are recalled more frequently than words in the middle.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly explains why words at the beginning of the list are remembered better than those in the middle?

choices.text: Recency effect — the words at the beginning of the list were studied most recently before the test, so they still remain active in working memory at retrieval time
choices.explanation: The recency effect explains better recall of the last items (which remain in working memory at test time) — the first items are recalled better for a different reason (long-term transfer via rehearsal), which is the primacy effect.

choices.text: Retroactive interference — early words are uniquely unaffected because later-studied words selectively overwrite only the middle and most recent list items
choices.explanation: Retroactive interference is when newer learning disrupts older memories — the primacy effect occurs because early items were well-rehearsed into LTM before interference; it's not that later items 'skip over' early ones.

choices.text: Primacy effect — early words received more rehearsal time and were transferred to long-term memory before interference built up
choices.explanation: The primacy effect occurs because early list items are rehearsed more times before subsequent items create interference, allowing transfer to long-term memory — making them more retrievable at test.

choices.text: Proactive interference — early words actively interfere with the encoding of subsequent list items, which is what makes the early items themselves more distinctive and therefore more memorable
choices.explanation: Proactive interference is when earlier learning disrupts later learning — while it does impair middle-list item encoding, the superior recall of early items is primarily due to greater rehearsal opportunity, not because they interfere with others.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: A student who learned French in middle school finds that her French vocabulary interferes with her current efforts to learn Spanish. Which type of forgetting best describes this?

choices.text: Motivated forgetting
choices.explanation: Motivated forgetting (repression) involves unconsciously pushing threatening or distressing memories out of awareness — learning a new language is not emotionally threatening in this context.

choices.text: Retroactive interference
choices.explanation: Retroactive interference is when new learning disrupts old memories — Spanish interfering with French recall would be retroactive; the reverse (French interfering with Spanish) is proactive.

choices.text: Encoding failure
choices.explanation: Encoding failure occurs when information is never properly encoded into memory in the first place — the student does remember her French (it's the interference source), so encoding failure does not explain this phenomenon.

choices.text: Proactive interference
choices.explanation: Proactive interference occurs when older previously learned information disrupts the learning or recall of newer information — French (older learning) is interfering with Spanish (newer learning).

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: A behaviorist trains pigeons to peck a key whenever it is illuminated. He then introduces a darker-colored key. The pigeons peck the new key at a lower rate than the original bright key, but still peck it. When the two keys are presented simultaneously and the pigeons learn to peck only the original bright key, they receive food only for that response.

questions.question: The pigeons learning to peck only the original bright key in the simultaneous condition best illustrates which classical or operant conditioning concept?

choices.text: Extinction of the original conditioned response
choices.explanation: Extinction occurs when a conditioned response decreases because the unconditioned stimulus (food) is no longer paired with the conditioned stimulus — food is still presented for correct responses; what occurs is discrimination training, not extinction.

choices.text: Stimulus generalization to the darker key
choices.explanation: Stimulus generalization is demonstrated earlier in the scenario (pecking the darker key at some rate) — the final phase where the pigeon learns to peck only the bright key is discrimination (the opposite of generalization).

choices.text: Stimulus discrimination through differential reinforcement
choices.explanation: Stimulus discrimination occurs when an organism learns to respond to one specific stimulus and not to similar stimuli — presenting both keys and reinforcing only the original bright key trains the pigeon to discriminate between them through differential reinforcement.

choices.text: Spontaneous recovery following discrimination training
choices.explanation: Spontaneous recovery is the reappearance of an extinguished response after a rest period — this scenario involves active discrimination training with differential reinforcement, not recovery of an extinguished response.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: In a famous experiment, children watched an adult model punch, kick, and scream at an inflatable Bobo doll. When later placed in a room with the Bobo doll, the children who had observed the aggressive adult displayed similar aggressive behaviors toward the doll, even without receiving any reinforcement for doing so.

questions.question: Which concept did this experiment most directly demonstrate, and who was the researcher?

choices.text: Observational learning; Albert Bandura
choices.explanation: Bandura's Bobo doll experiment demonstrated that behavior can be learned through observation and imitation without direct reinforcement — establishing observational (social) learning as a key mechanism of behavioral acquisition.

choices.text: Latent learning; Edward Tolman
choices.explanation: Latent learning occurs when learning happens without obvious motivation or reinforcement, stored for later use — Tolman studied this in rats navigating mazes; Bandura's Bobo doll study demonstrated observational social learning.

choices.text: Operant conditioning; B.F. Skinner
choices.explanation: Operant conditioning requires the learner to receive direct reinforcement or punishment — the children did not receive reinforcement for their aggressive behavior; they learned through observation alone.

choices.text: Classical conditioning; Ivan Pavlov
choices.explanation: Classical conditioning involves learning through paired stimulus associations — the children were not conditioned through pairing; they learned by watching a model, which is observational learning.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: A rat is placed in a maze with food at the end. For the first 10 days, the rat wanders the maze slowly and appears to explore without urgency. On day 11, food is introduced, and the rat immediately runs the maze much faster than a control rat that has been reinforced with food from day 1.

questions.question: This pattern of results most directly demonstrates which type of learning, and what does it suggest about how the rat was learning during the first 10 days?

choices.text: Classical conditioning — the maze environment functioned as a conditioned stimulus that paired with exploratory movement to trigger systematic spatial searching behaviors through Pavlovian associations
choices.explanation: Classical conditioning would involve the maze becoming associated with a biologically significant stimulus — the rat's exploratory behavior shows spatial learning without reinforcement, which is latent learning, not CS-CR association.

choices.text: Insight learning — the introduction of food triggered a sudden Kohler-type restructuring, allowing the rat to grasp the maze solution instantaneously without benefiting from its prior 10 days of spatial exploration
choices.explanation: Insight learning (Kohler) involves suddenly understanding a solution without relevant prior practice — the 10 days of maze exploration provided direct spatial experience; the rapid performance shows accumulated spatial knowledge (latent learning), not a sudden novel insight.

choices.text: Observational learning — through Bandura's modeling process, the rat learned the entire maze layout by watching researchers place and retrieve the food reward across multiple sessions
choices.explanation: Observational learning involves watching another organism perform a behavior — the rat explored the maze alone; the learning here occurred through direct environmental experience stored as a cognitive map.

choices.text: Latent learning — the rat was building a cognitive map of the maze during exploration, storing information that was only expressed when motivated by food
choices.explanation: Tolman's concept of latent learning holds that organisms learn from experience even without reinforcement, storing information in cognitive maps — the rat's rapid performance after food was introduced shows this stored knowledge, not new learning.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: A student tries to recall a professor's name but cannot, although she knows she has met her before. Later that evening, the name suddenly comes to her without any effort. This phenomenon is best described as which of the following?

choices.text: The tip-of-the-tongue phenomenon followed by delayed retrieval
choices.explanation: The tip-of-the-tongue phenomenon describes knowing you know a word or name but being unable to retrieve it immediately — the subsequent spontaneous recall demonstrates the information was in memory all along, requiring only the right retrieval conditions.

choices.text: Encoding failure — the name was never properly stored, so it could not be retrieved
choices.explanation: Encoding failure means the information was never stored in memory — but the student did eventually recall the name, proving it was stored; the issue was temporary retrieval failure, not encoding failure.

choices.text: Retroactive interference, cleared when competing memories faded
choices.explanation: Retroactive interference involves new learning disrupting old memories — this scenario describes temporary retrieval difficulty followed by spontaneous recall, not a process where competing new memories were blocking access.

choices.text: Long-term potentiation occurring hours after the initial encoding
choices.explanation: Long-term potentiation is a cellular synaptic strengthening process that occurs as information is encoded — it does not explain the delayed retrieval of previously encoded information that the student clearly already possessed.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: A person is presented with a random sequence of items: guitar, umbrella, phone, jacket, table. After hearing the list, she is asked to recall as many items as possible in any order. She correctly recalls 'guitar' and 'table,' but misses the middle items.

questions.question: The pattern of her recall is most consistent with which phenomenon?

choices.text: State-dependent memory — recall is best for items encoded during attentional states matching the recall state
choices.explanation: State-dependent memory predicts that recall is best when retrieval conditions match encoding conditions — it does not predict a position-based recall pattern, which is the serial position effect.

choices.text: Chunking failure — the random items could not be organized into memorable groups
choices.explanation: Chunking failure would predict uniformly poor recall across the list — the position-specific pattern (first and last items better than middle) is explained by the serial position effect, not inability to chunk.

choices.text: The serial position effect — better recall of items at the beginning (primacy) and end (recency) of a list
choices.explanation: The serial position effect describes the U-shaped recall curve where the first items (primacy effect) and last items (recency effect) are remembered better than middle items — the person's pattern of remembering 'guitar' (first) and 'table' (last) while forgetting middle items is the classic demonstration.

choices.text: Proactive interference — later items interfere with recall of earlier items
choices.explanation: Proactive interference would predict worse recall for later items because earlier items interfere with them — but the person does recall the last item ('table'), contradicting proactive interference as the explanation.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: A patient who suffered a severe head injury is unable to form new long-term explicit memories. However, she can still play the piano, which she learned before the injury, and she gradually improves at a new task of reading mirror-reversed text, even though she has no conscious memory of practicing it.

questions.question: The preservation of the patient's piano playing and improvement in mirror reading most directly illustrates which distinction?

choices.text: The distinction between episodic and semantic memory — the head injury selectively erased personal autobiographical memories while leaving intact all factual knowledge and conceptual understanding the patient had acquired
choices.explanation: Episodic and semantic memory are both forms of explicit memory — the patient's preserved abilities (piano, mirror reading) are implicit/procedural memories, not factual knowledge; this distinction is not relevant here.

choices.text: State-dependent memory — the patient can retrieve new explicit memories when she is returned to the specific emotional and physiological state in which the information was originally encoded, explaining the selective forgetting pattern
choices.explanation: State-dependent memory involves context matching for retrieval — the patient has a persistent neurological deficit preventing new explicit memory formation; this is not a retrieval state issue.

choices.text: The levels of processing effect — the patient's piano playing and mirror-reading skills resist forgetting because they were encoded at the deepest meaningful semantic level, creating stronger and more durable memory traces than explicit verbal information
choices.explanation: Levels of processing predicts deeper semantic encoding produces stronger memories — but piano playing is not a 'deeply encoded semantic' memory; it is an implicit procedural skill. The explanation for its preservation is the involvement of different brain systems, not encoding depth.

choices.text: The dissociation between implicit (procedural) memory and explicit (declarative) memory — the hippocampus-dependent explicit system is damaged while the implicit system is intact
choices.explanation: Motor skills (piano) and procedural learning (mirror reading improvement) are stored as implicit memory in the cerebellum and basal ganglia — independent of the hippocampus. The patient's inability to form new explicit memories with preserved procedural learning demonstrates this double dissociation.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: When asked to estimate the number of words in the English language that begin with the letter 'K,' most people estimate a much larger number than the number of words that have 'K' as the third letter, even though there are actually more words with 'K' in the third position.

questions.question: This cognitive error is best explained by which concept?

choices.text: Availability heuristic — words beginning with 'K' are easier to think of, making people overestimate their frequency
choices.explanation: The availability heuristic leads people to judge frequency or probability by how easily examples come to mind — 'K'-initial words (king, knife, know) are easier to retrieve than words with 'K' in the third position (bike, like, take), causing an overestimation of K-initial word frequency.

choices.text: Representativeness heuristic — words beginning with 'K' better match the prototype of 'K words'
choices.explanation: The representativeness heuristic involves judging likelihood based on how well something matches a category prototype — this scenario involves frequency estimation based on ease of recall, which is the availability heuristic, not representativeness.

choices.text: Confirmation bias — people only search for words beginning with 'K' because they expect that category to be larger
choices.explanation: Confirmation bias involves selectively seeking information that confirms existing beliefs — the estimation error here is caused by differential memory accessibility (availability), not by selectively confirming a pre-existing belief about K-words.

choices.text: Functional fixedness — people are fixed on the typical starting position of letters in words
choices.explanation: Functional fixedness is about being unable to see novel uses for objects — it is a problem-solving limitation, not a frequency estimation bias based on memory accessibility.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: A woman always uses the same route to drive to work. One day the road is blocked, and she has difficulty finding an alternative route, even though there are several efficient alternatives. When a passenger suggests using a parallel street she has never considered, the woman realizes it is obviously the best alternative.

questions.question: The woman's initial difficulty in finding an alternative route is best explained by which of the following?

choices.text: Mental set — her habitual approach to the commute created a fixed cognitive pattern that made alternative routes difficult to consider
choices.explanation: Mental set is the tendency to approach a problem with the same strategy that has worked before, even when a different approach would be better — the woman's established commute routine made her cognitively fixed on that approach, hindering flexible problem-solving.

choices.text: Retroactive interference — recently repeated use of the habitual route created new memory traces that blocked her access to older alternative route memories stored in long-term memory
choices.explanation: Retroactive interference involves new learning disrupting old memories — the woman's difficulty is not about memory retrieval of known routes; she lacks awareness of the alternative, which points to mental set, not memory interference.

choices.text: Functional fixedness — the woman perceived her car only as a tool for one specific predefined route and could not conceptualize it serving the function of transporting her via any alternative road network
choices.explanation: Functional fixedness involves being unable to see novel uses for an object (e.g., using a pen as a lever) — the woman's problem is about cognitive rigidity in approach (mental set), not about failing to see a novel function for the car.

choices.text: Divergent thinking failure — the woman's convergent problem-solving style completely dominated her cognitive approach, making it impossible for her to generate even a single alternative route without external prompting
choices.explanation: Divergent thinking involves generating multiple creative solutions — the woman's problem is specifically the cognitive rigidity from habit (mental set), not a general failure of creative thinking; once prompted, she immediately saw the alternative.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Which perceptual process involves interpreting incoming sensory information based on prior knowledge, expectations, and context?

choices.text: Selective attention
choices.explanation: Selective attention is the process of focusing on one stimulus while filtering out others — it is an attentional, not perceptual interpretation, process.

choices.text: Bottom-up processing
choices.explanation: Bottom-up processing builds perception from raw sensory data upward without the influence of prior expectations — the opposite of top-down processing.

choices.text: Signal detection
choices.explanation: Signal detection theory addresses how psychological factors affect the ability to detect a stimulus — it is a theory of threshold measurement, not a description of how prior knowledge shapes perception.

choices.text: Top-down processing
choices.explanation: Top-down processing uses existing knowledge, expectations, and context to interpret sensory information — the brain actively shapes perception based on what it knows, rather than passively receiving input.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: A participant in a psychology experiment is watching a video of people passing a basketball. The participant is asked to count the number of times the ball is passed. Halfway through the video, a person in a gorilla suit walks through the scene. When asked afterward, the participant reports not seeing the gorilla.

questions.question: Which phenomenon does this experiment best illustrate?

choices.text: The cocktail party effect — the participant focused all attention on the basketball task the same way one focuses on a single voice amid competing party noise, filtering out all other visual stimuli
choices.explanation: The cocktail party effect describes selective attention in auditory contexts — filtering out background noise to focus on one conversation; the gorilla experiment involves visual inattentional blindness, a different phenomenon.

choices.text: Inattentional blindness — attention was focused on the basketball task, preventing conscious perception of the unexpected gorilla
choices.explanation: Inattentional blindness is the failure to notice an unexpected stimulus when attention is fully engaged elsewhere — the counting task occupied all attentional resources, causing the gorilla to go unnoticed despite being in plain sight.

choices.text: Perceptual set — the participant's schema for basketball-related stimuli so strongly filtered incoming visual data that the gorilla's image was categorically excluded from perceptual processing before reaching conscious awareness
choices.explanation: Perceptual set involves prior expectations shaping perception — while related, the specific mechanism here is attentional capacity being occupied by the counting task (inattentional blindness), not simply a schema mismatch from perceptual set.

choices.text: Change blindness — the video editing produced scene cuts during which the gorilla was introduced, and the participant's visual system failed to detect the change across those discontinuous frames as in classic change blindness paradigms
choices.explanation: Change blindness is the failure to notice a difference between two successive views of the same scene — the gorilla was present continuously in a single uncut scene; the participant's failure to notice it despite it being unexpected and in motion is inattentional blindness.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: An artist looking at a landscape scene uses the size of distant mountains relative to nearby trees, the haziness of far-away hills, and the apparent convergence of a road toward the horizon to judge distance.

questions.question: Which type of depth cues is the artist using, and how many eyes are required to use them?

choices.text: Monocular cues requiring convergence — both eyes turn inward to perceive distance
choices.explanation: Convergence (eyes turning inward) is a binocular cue that applies for nearby objects — monocular depth cues like relative size and atmospheric perspective do not require eye convergence.

choices.text: Binocular depth cues — both eyes work together to perceive the differences in mountain size and road convergence
choices.explanation: Binocular cues (retinal disparity and convergence) require both eyes and are primarily useful for objects within arm's reach — the landscape features described are monocular cues effective at great distances.

choices.text: Gestalt grouping principles — proximity and similarity allow the artist to perceive landscape depth
choices.explanation: Gestalt grouping principles (proximity, similarity, closure, figure/ground) describe how elements are organized into wholes — they are organizational principles, not depth cues for perceiving distance.

choices.text: Monocular depth cues — only one eye is required to perceive relative size, relative clarity, and linear perspective
choices.explanation: Relative size, relative clarity (atmospheric perspective), and linear perspective (convergence) are all monocular cues — they provide depth information from a single eye's view without needing the comparison between two eyes' slightly different images.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: Alan Baddeley and Graham Hitch proposed a working memory model to replace the unitary short-term memory concept in the Atkinson-Shiffrin model. Their model includes a central executive and two 'slave systems.'

questions.question: A student simultaneously listens to a podcast while reading a textbook. Which aspect of the working memory model best explains why this dual-task situation is likely to impair comprehension of both sources?

choices.text: Both tasks compete for limited central executive resources, and the phonological loop handles the podcast while the visuospatial sketchpad handles reading — but the central executive cannot effectively coordinate both simultaneously
choices.explanation: The central executive has limited attentional resources — while the podcast uses the phonological loop and reading uses the visuospatial sketchpad, the central executive must coordinate both systems and allocate attention; dividing it between two demanding tasks reduces comprehension for both.

choices.text: Long-term potentiation between the hippocampal memory system and the cortex fails catastrophically when two competing information streams arrive simultaneously, physically blocking memory consolidation for both the podcast and textbook content
choices.explanation: Long-term potentiation is a cellular memory consolidation mechanism — the working memory model describes active information processing, not LTP; the dual-task impairment is an executive attention issue, not a synaptic one.

choices.text: Both the podcast's auditory narrative and the textbook's visual content compete for space within the visuospatial sketchpad, which must simultaneously handle both symbolic and spatial processing demands, creating mutual interference between the two inputs
choices.explanation: The podcast (auditory) is processed by the phonological loop, not the visuospatial sketchpad — the sketchpad handles visual-spatial information (reading); the key bottleneck is the central executive coordinating both.

choices.text: The phonological loop reaches and exceeds its 7±2 chunk capacity limit when required to simultaneously maintain the podcast's verbal content and the textbook's written text, causing the excess information to be displaced and permanently lost before encoding
choices.explanation: The 7±2 chunk limit is Miller's estimate of short-term memory capacity — the phonological loop stores verbal information, and the relevant impairment comes from central executive overload, not phonological loop capacity.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: A child knows that dogs bark and are furry, so when she sees a cat for the first time, she calls it a 'doggie.' Which cognitive process does this illustrate?

choices.text: Top-down processing — prior knowledge of dogs is applied to the new visual input
choices.explanation: Top-down processing involves prior knowledge shaping perception — while related, the question specifically tests Piaget's concept of assimilation (applying an existing schema), not the broader perceptual processing concept.

choices.text: Accommodation — modifying the existing 'dog' schema to include cats
choices.explanation: Accommodation involves adjusting or creating a schema to fit new information — this would occur if the child corrected herself and created a separate 'cat' category; calling the cat a 'doggie' shows assimilation, not yet accommodation.

choices.text: Assimilation — fitting the new cat into the existing 'dog' schema
choices.explanation: Assimilation is interpreting new experiences through existing schemas — the child applies her 'dog' schema (furry, four-legged animal) to the cat, fitting the new object into a familiar mental framework.

choices.text: Representativeness heuristic — the cat resembles the prototype for 'dog,' so it is categorized as one
choices.explanation: The representativeness heuristic is about judging probability of category membership based on prototype similarity — while overlapping, the specific Piagetian developmental process being tested here is assimilation.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: A study tested whether the way a decision is framed affects people's choices. In the 'lives saved' frame, participants chose between: Option A: Save 200 people for certain; Option B: 1/3 chance of saving 600 people, 2/3 chance of saving no one. In the 'lives lost' frame, participants chose between: Option C: 400 people will die for certain; Option D: 1/3 chance of no one dying, 2/3 chance that all 600 will die. Options A and C are objectively identical, as are B and D.

questions.question: The finding that most people prefer Option A when presented with the 'lives saved' frame but prefer Option D when presented with the 'lives lost' frame most directly illustrates which concept?

choices.text: Sunk-cost fallacy — participants who previously committed to saving people continue preferring Option A regardless of how the probabilities are presented, because their prior commitment creates an irrational investment effect
choices.explanation: The sunk-cost fallacy involves continuing a losing course because of prior investment — there is no prior investment in this scenario; the effect is about how the framing of the same options as gains versus losses changes the preference.

choices.text: The representativeness heuristic — people judge Option A as more probable and better because 200 certain survivors better matches the prototype of an effective rescue plan than an uncertain probabilistic gamble involving 600 people
choices.explanation: The representativeness heuristic involves judging probability by prototype similarity — the choice shift between frames is about how losses versus gains are evaluated differently, which is the framing effect.

choices.text: The framing effect — the same objective information leads to different choices depending on whether it is framed in terms of gains (lives saved) or losses
choices.explanation: This is the classic Kahneman and Tversky framing effect demonstration — people are risk-averse when outcomes are framed as gains (preferring the certain option A) but risk-seeking when the same outcomes are framed as losses (preferring the gamble D).

choices.text: Confirmation bias — participants in both frames selectively process information that confirms their pre-existing moral beliefs about whether saving or risking lives is more ethical, producing the apparent preference reversal across conditions
choices.explanation: Confirmation bias involves seeking information that confirms prior beliefs — the participants in both frames are making identical choices mathematically; the preference reversal based on framing is the framing effect, not confirmation of pre-existing beliefs.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: A 2-year-old child says 'more milk' when she wants more milk and 'daddy go' when her father leaves for work. Her sentences consist of just the most essential content words, omitting articles, prepositions, and verb endings.

questions.question: This stage of language development is best described as which of the following?

choices.text: Overgeneralization — the child is incorrectly applying grammatical morpheme rules to content words she has not fully mastered yet
choices.explanation: Overgeneralization involves applying regular grammatical rules incorrectly to irregular forms (e.g., 'goed' for 'went') — the child's pattern of omitting function words is telegraphic speech, not a rule application error.

choices.text: Babbling — the child is producing repetitive consonant-vowel syllables like 'da-da' or 'ma-ma' that lack referential semantic content or communicative intent
choices.explanation: Babbling consists of meaningless consonant-vowel repetitions (e.g., 'ba-ba-ba') — the child here is using meaningful two-word utterances with communicative intent, well past the babbling stage.

choices.text: Telegraphic speech — two-word utterances that convey essential meaning while omitting grammatical function words
choices.explanation: Telegraphic speech (approximately 18–24 months) consists of two-word combinations that carry the most important semantic content while omitting grammatical function words (articles, prepositions, inflections) — matching this child's pattern.

choices.text: One-word stage (holophrastic speech) — the child uses single content words like 'milk' or 'daddy' to holistically express complex communicative intentions
choices.explanation: The one-word (holophrastic) stage involves single words — 'more,' not 'more milk'; the child here uses two-word combinations, placing her in the telegraphic speech stage.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: A dog trainer rings a bell every time she feeds her dog. After several weeks, the dog begins to salivate whenever it hears the bell, even before food is presented. The trainer then rings the bell 50 times in a row without presenting any food.

questions.question: If the dog stopped salivating to the bell after the 50 unreinforced trials, but then salivated again to the bell after a 2-week break, which classical conditioning phenomenon would this represent?

choices.text: Stimulus generalization — the rest period caused the dog to begin responding to other bell-like sounds or tones as well as the original CS bell
choices.explanation: Stimulus generalization involves responding to stimuli similar to the original CS — spontaneous recovery specifically involves the reappearance of the CR to the original CS after a rest period following extinction.

choices.text: Spontaneous recovery — the extinguished CR reappears after a rest period without additional conditioning trials
choices.explanation: Spontaneous recovery is the reappearance of a conditioned response after extinction and a rest period, demonstrating that extinction suppresses rather than eliminates the conditioned association.

choices.text: Reconditioning — the trainer inadvertently presented the bell alongside food-related cues during the rest period, re-establishing the bell-food classical conditioning association
choices.explanation: Reconditioning would require repairing the bell-food association through additional trials — spontaneous recovery occurs without any additional training; the CR returns simply due to the passage of time.

choices.text: Second-order conditioning — during the rest period, new environmental stimuli in the room were automatically paired with the bell, creating new higher-order conditioned associations that restored salivation
choices.explanation: Second-order conditioning occurs when a new neutral stimulus is paired with an established CS to produce a conditioned response — the rest period scenario describes spontaneous recovery, not new higher-order conditioning.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: In a study, participants were asked to generate as many uses as possible for a brick in 3 minutes. One group was given a list of 5 common uses to begin with; another group started from scratch. The group given the initial common-use list generated significantly fewer unique, creative uses than the group that started with no initial list.

questions.question: The reduced creative performance in the group given the initial list is most directly explained by which concept?

choices.text: Mental set — the initial list primed a fixed, conventional approach to thinking about brick uses, inhibiting divergent thinking
choices.explanation: Mental set creates cognitive rigidity — the common-use list activated habitual, conventional ways of thinking about bricks, making it harder for participants to break free and generate creative novel uses (divergent thinking).

choices.text: The availability heuristic — participants who saw common uses first judged those uses as more numerous and therefore better answers, causing them to persist in conventional rather than creative directions
choices.explanation: The availability heuristic involves frequency/probability judgment based on ease of recall — the study measured creative output quantity and quality, not frequency estimates; the cognitive rigidity from the initial list is mental set.

choices.text: Proactive interference — the initial list of conventional uses, now in short-term memory, actively disrupted participants' ability to retrieve creative alternative uses from long-term memory storage
choices.explanation: Proactive interference involves older information disrupting recall of newer information — the initial list was provided immediately before the task; the interference is on ideation (generating new ideas), not on recalling stored memories.

choices.text: Functional fixedness — participants who received the list became cognitively locked into perceiving bricks only as building materials, making it impossible to conceptualize any non-construction applications throughout the 3-minute period
choices.explanation: Functional fixedness specifically concerns the inability to see objects beyond their typical function — the initial list actually included varied uses; mental set (the primed conventional approach from seeing examples) better explains the reduced creative output.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: A person crossing the street sees the same black car that passed earlier approaching again. Although the car is farther away and projects a smaller image on her retina, she does not perceive it as having shrunk.

questions.question: Which perceptual principle best explains her accurate perception of the car's size?

choices.text: Bottom-up processing — raw retinal data is processed without prior knowledge, and the car's retinal image size is unchanged
choices.explanation: Bottom-up processing would actually be problematic here — the retinal image IS smaller from a distance; size constancy requires top-down knowledge (knowing what the car is and that it is the same car) to override the retinal change, which is the opposite of pure bottom-up processing.

choices.text: Relative size — the car appears the same size because it is compared to the same surrounding objects
choices.explanation: Relative size is a depth cue using size comparisons to estimate distance — while it helps perceive depth, size constancy is the specific principle explaining why familiar objects maintain their perceived size despite retinal image changes.

choices.text: Figure-ground organization — the car remains the figure against a background, preserving its perceived size
choices.explanation: Figure-ground is a Gestalt principle separating elements into foreground and background — it does not explain how perceived size remains constant despite changes in retinal image size as distance changes.

choices.text: Size constancy — the brain compensates for retinal image size changes using distance information to maintain accurate perceived size
choices.explanation: Size constancy is the perceptual stability that keeps known objects appearing their actual size regardless of retinal image changes — the brain uses distance cues to adjust perceived size, keeping the car's perceived size constant.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: A psychologist interested in cognition finds that expert chess players can memorize the positions of chess pieces on a board after just a 5-second viewing, while beginners can remember only 4–5 pieces from the same view. However, when the pieces are placed randomly (not in game-legal positions), the expert advantage disappears and both groups remember similarly.

questions.question: Which explanation best accounts for the expert advantage with game-legal board positions?

choices.text: Higher innate intelligence — chess experts possess genetically superior working memory capacity and faster processing speed, giving them an advantage in short-term retention of any type of visual pattern including random chess configurations
choices.explanation: The expert advantage disappears with random positions — if it were due to innately larger memory capacity, experts would remember more pieces regardless of configuration; the advantage is position-pattern specific, confirming it is chunking, not general capacity.

choices.text: Long-term potentiation — thousands of hours of chess exposure have strengthened hippocampal synaptic connections governing visual-spatial memory, increasing the amount of board information experts can retain after 5 seconds of viewing
choices.explanation: LTP is a cellular mechanism for memory formation — while it underlies learning, it does not specifically explain the position-dependent expert advantage; chunking (organizing information into meaningful units) is the directly applicable concept.

choices.text: Chunking — experts recognize meaningful patterns (clusters of related pieces) as single memory units, allowing them to remember more pieces within the same capacity limit
choices.explanation: Experts chunk game-meaningful clusters of pieces into single memory units — what a novice sees as 16 separate pieces, an expert perceives as 4–5 meaningful attack/defense formations (chunks), allowing much more to be stored within the same working memory capacity.

choices.text: Levels of processing — because chess is personally significant and emotionally meaningful to experts, they automatically encode board positions at a deeper semantic level, producing stronger and more durable short-term visual memories for any board configuration
choices.explanation: While expertise involves deeper encoding, the specific mechanism explaining position-specific advantage is chunking — encoding meaningful patterns as units; levels of processing alone would not explain why the expert advantage disappears with random positions.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: According to Baddeley's working memory model, which component is responsible for coordinating the activities of the phonological loop and visuospatial sketchpad?

choices.text: Central executive
choices.explanation: The central executive is the supervisory component of working memory — it allocates attention, coordinates the two slave systems (phonological loop and visuospatial sketchpad), and manages the interface between working memory and long-term memory.

choices.text: Episodic buffer
choices.explanation: The episodic buffer (added later by Baddeley) integrates information from the slave systems and long-term memory into a unified episode — it does not directly coordinate the other components; the central executive serves the coordination function.

choices.text: Visuospatial sketchpad
choices.explanation: The visuospatial sketchpad stores and manipulates visual and spatial information — it is the other slave system, also coordinated by the central executive, not the executive itself.

choices.text: Phonological loop
choices.explanation: The phonological loop stores and rehearses verbal/acoustic information — it is one of the slave systems coordinated by the central executive, not the coordinator itself.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Which of the following best describes the process of classical conditioning?

choices.text: A behavior is strengthened or weakened by its consequences through reinforcement or punishment
choices.explanation: Behavior strengthened or weakened by consequences describes operant conditioning (Skinner) — not classical conditioning, which involves stimulus-stimulus pairing, not behavior-consequence relationships.

choices.text: New information is encoded through semantic processing and stored in long-term memory
choices.explanation: Semantic encoding and long-term storage describe memory processes — classical conditioning is a form of associative learning between stimuli, not memory encoding of information.

choices.text: A neutral stimulus becomes associated with an unconditioned stimulus and elicits a conditioned response
choices.explanation: In classical conditioning (Pavlov), a neutral stimulus (e.g., a bell) is paired repeatedly with an unconditioned stimulus (e.g., food) until the neutral stimulus alone elicits a conditioned response (e.g., salivation).

choices.text: A person learns a new behavior by observing and imitating others without direct reinforcement
choices.explanation: Learning by observing and imitating others describes observational (social) learning (Bandura) — classical conditioning involves automatic associations between stimuli, not modeled behavior.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: According to George Miller, the capacity of short-term (working) memory is approximately how many items?

choices.text: About 20 items when the rehearsal loop is used
choices.explanation: The phonological loop (rehearsal) helps maintain items in working memory but does not expand capacity to 20 items — the fundamental capacity limit remains approximately 7 +/- 2 chunks.

choices.text: 3 to 4 items (subitizing limit)
choices.explanation: Subitizing (instantly perceiving 3-4 items) is a perceptual phenomenon — Miller identified the short-term memory capacity as approximately 7 +/- 2 chunks, not 3-4 items.

choices.text: 7 plus or minus 2 items
choices.explanation: George Miller's classic 1956 paper 'The Magical Number Seven, Plus or Minus Two' demonstrated that short-term memory holds approximately 5-9 chunks of information — commonly summarized as 7 +/- 2.

choices.text: Unlimited items as long as they are semantically related
choices.explanation: Unlimited capacity describes long-term memory, not working memory — working memory is severely capacity-limited regardless of semantic relatedness, though chunking related items can increase effective capacity.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: A student who studied French vocabulary in high school finds that learning Spanish vocabulary in college is more difficult because the French words keep coming to mind when she tries to recall Spanish words.

questions.question: Which memory phenomenon best explains the student's difficulty?

choices.text: State-dependent memory — the student learned French in a different emotional state than Spanish
choices.explanation: State-dependent memory refers to retrieval being easier in the same physiological or emotional state as encoding — the scenario describes linguistic interference between two learned languages, not state matching.

choices.text: The misinformation effect — the French words are false memories inserted by the student's teacher
choices.explanation: The misinformation effect involves post-event information altering an existing memory — here, French vocabulary is genuinely learned knowledge that is interfering with, not falsifying, the Spanish vocabulary.

choices.text: Proactive interference — previously learned French is disrupting the retrieval of newly learned Spanish
choices.explanation: Proactive interference occurs when older memories interfere with the recall of newer ones — the student's previously learned French intrudes on retrieval of newly learned Spanish, a classic proactive interference pattern.

choices.text: Retroactive interference — newly learned Spanish is disrupting retrieval of previously learned French
choices.explanation: Retroactive interference occurs when newer learning disrupts recall of older memories — the student is having trouble recalling Spanish (new) because French (old) intrudes; this is proactive, not retroactive, interference.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: A child learns to fear dogs after being bitten by a large dog. Later, the child also shows fear responses to cats, rabbits, and other small animals, even though she has never had a negative experience with them.

questions.question: Which classical conditioning process does the child's fear of cats and rabbits best illustrate?

choices.text: Stimulus discrimination — the child has learned to distinguish between dangerous and harmless animals
choices.explanation: Stimulus discrimination is the opposite — it is the learned ability to respond only to the original CS and not to similar stimuli. The child is showing generalization (fear spreading to other animals), not discrimination.

choices.text: Stimulus generalization — conditioned fear spreads to stimuli similar to the original conditioned stimulus
choices.explanation: Stimulus generalization occurs when a conditioned response elicited by a CS also appears in response to similar stimuli — the child's fear generalizes from dogs (CS) to other small animals that share similar features.

choices.text: Extinction — the original conditioned response is being weakened by repeated non-reinforced presentations
choices.explanation: Extinction requires repeated presentation of the CS without the UCS, causing the CR to fade — the child's fear is not diminishing; it is spreading to new stimuli, which is generalization.

choices.text: Second-order conditioning — the cats and rabbits have been paired with the dog, creating a chain of associations
choices.explanation: Second-order conditioning requires explicit pairing of a new neutral stimulus with an existing CS — the child has not paired cats or rabbits with dogs; fear has generalized automatically to similar stimuli.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: A student is studying by writing each vocabulary term three times, forming a sentence with the word, and then explaining how the term relates to a real-world example she personally experienced. A classmate reads the same terms five times without additional processing.

questions.question: Based on memory research, whose recall will most likely be superior one week later, and why?

choices.text: The first student's, because elaborative rehearsal and semantic encoding create deeper memory traces than rote repetition
choices.explanation: Levels of processing theory predicts deeper encoding (meaning-based, personal connection) produces more durable memories. Elaborative rehearsal — forming meaningful associations — produces stronger long-term retention than maintenance rehearsal (simple repetition).

choices.text: The classmate's, because repeated exposure (five repetitions) creates stronger automatic processing paths than elaborative strategies
choices.explanation: Repetition alone (maintenance rehearsal) is less effective for long-term retention than elaborative rehearsal — research consistently shows semantic processing produces superior recall compared to rote repetition.

choices.text: The classmate's, because reading silently avoids auditory interference that disrupts the phonological loop
choices.explanation: Avoiding phonological loop interference would not make rote reading superior to elaborative encoding — the phonological loop supports short-term maintenance, not long-term consolidation, which depends on depth of processing.

choices.text: Both equally, because memory capacity is fixed and the method of encoding has no effect on retention strength
choices.explanation: Memory encoding method strongly affects retention — this is the core finding of levels of processing research (Craik and Lockhart). Deeper, meaning-based encoding produces reliably superior long-term recall.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: A gambler continues to play slot machines even after losing large sums of money, convinced that 'the machine is due for a big payout.' In reality, each spin is independent of all previous spins.

questions.question: Which cognitive bias best explains the gambler's reasoning?

choices.text: Confirmation bias — selectively attending to wins while ignoring losses to confirm the belief that the machine pays off
choices.explanation: Confirmation bias involves seeking or interpreting information to confirm existing beliefs — the gambler's fallacy is specifically about misunderstanding probability (believing past losses predict future wins), not about selective attention to confirming evidence.

choices.text: The gambler's fallacy — incorrectly believing that past independent random events influence future probabilities
choices.explanation: The gambler's fallacy is the mistaken belief that after a series of one outcome (e.g., losses), the opposite outcome becomes more likely — in reality, independent random events (like slot machine spins) have no memory of prior outcomes.

choices.text: The availability heuristic — the gambler easily recalls past wins, making winning seem more probable than it is
choices.explanation: The availability heuristic occurs when easily recalled examples make an event seem more likely — the gambler's reasoning is specifically about past losses making future wins seem 'due,' which is the gambler's fallacy, a distinct probability error.

choices.text: Functional fixedness — inability to see the slot machine as anything other than a money-generating device
choices.explanation: Functional fixedness is the inability to see objects as having uses other than their standard function — this gambler's error is a probability reasoning error (gambler's fallacy), not a problem with conceptualizing the machine's function.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: A researcher presents participants with two options: Option A — a guaranteed $50, or Option B — a 50% chance to win $100 and a 50% chance to win nothing. Most participants choose Option A. When the same options are framed as losses (Option A — a guaranteed loss of $50, or Option B — a 50% chance to lose $100, 50% chance to lose nothing), most participants choose Option B.

questions.question: Which cognitive phenomenon does this shift in preferences best illustrate?

choices.text: Mental set — participants are stuck in a problem-solving strategy from previous trials and cannot reconsider the options
choices.explanation: Mental set is a tendency to approach a new problem the same way as a previous one — the preference reversal here results from how options are framed (gains vs. losses), not from prior problem-solving habits.

choices.text: The representativeness heuristic — participants judge each option based on how well it represents a typical gamble
choices.explanation: The representativeness heuristic involves judging probability by similarity to a prototype — the gain vs. loss preference shift reflects the framing effect (emotional/motivational asymmetry), not prototype-based probability judgment.

choices.text: Framing effect — the way options are presented influences decision-making even when expected values are identical
choices.explanation: The framing effect demonstrates that decisions are influenced by whether options are presented as gains or losses, even when the mathematical expected values are identical. People are risk-averse for gains and risk-seeking for losses — a finding from Kahneman and Tversky's prospect theory.

choices.text: Anchoring bias — participants are anchored to the first option presented and fail to adjust their estimates
choices.explanation: Anchoring occurs when an initial value (the anchor) biases subsequent estimates — the shift in preference between gain and loss framing is the framing effect, not anchoring, which would produce responses biased toward a numerical anchor.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: A researcher wants to test whether rats can form cognitive maps. She allows one group of rats to freely explore a maze without any food reward for 10 days. A second group is rewarded with food from the first day. On day 11, both groups are rewarded with food. The previously exploring (non-rewarded) group reaches the food almost as quickly as the always-rewarded group.

questions.question: Which conclusion is best supported by these findings, and whose theoretical framework do they support?

choices.text: Rats form cognitive maps of their environment during unrewarded exploration — supporting Tolman's latent learning theory, which holds that learning can occur without reinforcement
choices.explanation: Edward Tolman's latent learning research showed that organisms learn about their environment even without immediate reward — the unexplored group had developed a mental map that enabled rapid performance once reward was introduced, demonstrating that learning can be latent (hidden) until motivation to use it emerges.

choices.text: The exploring rats' sudden performance improvement demonstrates negative reinforcement — the aversive discomfort of aimless exploration was removed by the food goal, and this relief from negative stimulation reinforced faster directed maze-running behavior
choices.explanation: Negative reinforcement involves removing an unpleasant stimulus to increase behavior — the rats' exploration was undirected and unrewarded; the rapid performance when food was introduced reflects a stored cognitive map, not relief from negative stimulation.

choices.text: The rats' rapid maze performance reflects instinctive drift — the introduction of food reward triggered powerful genetically programmed food-seeking behaviors that overrode and replaced the neutral exploratory responses learned during the first 10 days
choices.explanation: Instinctive drift describes how conditioned behaviors gradually revert to instinctive ones — the findings show the latently learned maze layout was applied purposefully, not that instinctive behaviors displaced learned responses.

choices.text: The findings support Pavlov's classical conditioning framework — through repeated exposure during exploration, the maze alleyways became conditioned stimuli that reliably predicted food arrival, producing anticipatory approach behaviors that appeared as faster running on day 11
choices.explanation: Classical conditioning would predict the maze triggers a conditioned response — but the dramatic performance difference requires explaining why non-rewarded exploration (without any CS-UCS pairing) still produced learning, which classical conditioning cannot account for.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: A participant in a study is asked to recall 20 words from a list she studied 30 minutes ago. She recalls 14 out of 20 words correctly. An analysis of her errors shows that 3 of the 6 forgotten words were from the middle of the list, while she correctly recalled nearly all words from the beginning and end of the list.

questions.question: Which memory principle best explains the pattern of her recall errors?

choices.text: The misinformation effect — the 30-minute delay allowed post-encoding interference to specifically target middle-list words
choices.explanation: The misinformation effect involves false post-event information altering memory — the pattern described here (poor middle recall, good beginning and end recall) is the serial position effect, not interference from misleading information.

choices.text: Serial position effect — words from the middle of the list lack both the encoding advantage of primacy and the retrieval advantage of recency
choices.explanation: The serial position effect predicts superior recall for items at the beginning (primacy — transferred to long-term memory through rehearsal) and end (recency — still in working memory) of a list. Middle items receive neither advantage, producing the worst recall — matching the participant's error pattern.

choices.text: Decay theory — middle-list words were encoded earliest and lost through simple decay before the recall test
choices.explanation: Decay theory predicts that earlier-encoded items would be lost first — but primacy words (early in the list) are recalled well, contradicting pure decay theory. The serial position effect better accounts for the specific beginning-end superiority.

choices.text: Context-dependent memory — the testing context differed from the encoding context, reducing recall of the emotionally neutral middle words
choices.explanation: Context-dependent memory predicts overall recall impairment when encoding and retrieval contexts differ — it does not specifically predict a position-based pattern of errors favoring the beginning and end of a list.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: Researchers study a patient (H.M.) who underwent bilateral hippocampal removal to treat severe epilepsy. After surgery, H.M. could not form new declarative memories — he could not recall new faces or events from minutes earlier. However, he could learn new motor skills (like mirror drawing) and showed normal improvement over sessions, even though he did not consciously remember practicing.

questions.question: Which conclusion about memory systems is best supported by H.M.'s case?

choices.text: H.M.'s preserved motor skill learning proves that the frontal lobe's executive control circuitry compensated for the bilateral hippocampal resection by assuming full responsibility for all forms of long-term memory storage and retrieval
choices.explanation: The frontal lobe manages executive functions and working memory — H.M.'s case shows procedural memory uses different systems (cerebellum, basal ganglia) than declarative memory, not that the frontal lobe compensated for hippocampal function.

choices.text: The hippocampus stores every variety of long-term memory, but H.M.'s preserved motor skill acquisition proves that short-term working memory has unlimited capacity to support skill learning as a compensatory mechanism when hippocampal function is lost
choices.explanation: Motor skill learning is long-term implicit memory — H.M.'s preserved skill learning shows the hippocampus is not required for all long-term memory; procedural memory uses different neural systems. This does not relate to short-term memory capacity.

choices.text: H.M.'s case demonstrates that all forms of declarative memory — both episodic and semantic — are stored in the cerebellum, which sustained collateral damage during the hippocampal resection surgery and thereby produced his profound amnesia
choices.explanation: The cerebellum mediates motor coordination and procedural skill learning — declarative memory depends on the hippocampus. H.M.'s cerebellar function was intact (as shown by his motor learning), while hippocampal removal eliminated his declarative memory formation.

choices.text: The hippocampus is essential for forming new explicit (declarative) memories but not for procedural (implicit) skill learning, suggesting these are separate memory systems
choices.explanation: H.M.'s preserved motor learning with impaired episodic/semantic memory formation demonstrates a double dissociation between explicit (hippocampus-dependent) and implicit procedural memory (cerebellum/basal ganglia-dependent) systems — a landmark finding supporting multi-store memory models.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a variable-ratio schedule of reinforcement?

choices.text: Receiving a paycheck every two weeks regardless of performance
choices.explanation: Receiving a paycheck on a fixed time schedule (every two weeks) describes a fixed-interval schedule — reinforcement is delivered after a predictable time period, not after an unpredictable number of responses.

choices.text: Getting a bonus after completing every 10 sales calls
choices.explanation: Reinforcement after every fixed number of responses describes a fixed-ratio schedule — the number of responses required is constant (10 calls), not variable.

choices.text: Being praised by a teacher at random times during the school day regardless of behavior
choices.explanation: Random praise based on time rather than number of responses describes a variable-interval schedule — reinforcement is delivered after an unpredictable time interval, not after an unpredictable number of behaviors.

choices.text: A slot machine that pays out after an unpredictable number of pulls
choices.explanation: A variable-ratio schedule delivers reinforcement after an unpredictable, average number of responses — slot machines are the classic example, producing high, steady rates of responding because the next reward could come at any time.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Which perceptual principle explains why a viewer sees the following pattern (**  **  **  **) as three pairs of stars rather than six separate stars?

choices.text: Gestalt principle of proximity — elements close together are perceived as belonging to the same group
choices.explanation: The Gestalt principle of proximity states that elements that are spatially near each other are perceptually grouped together — the pairs of stars appear grouped because stars within each pair are closer to each other than to stars in adjacent pairs.

choices.text: Gestalt principle of closure — the mind fills in gaps to create complete, familiar shapes from incomplete patterns
choices.explanation: Closure is the tendency to perceive incomplete figures as complete wholes — the stars are not an incomplete shape being closed; they are complete elements being grouped, which is proximity.

choices.text: Top-down processing — prior knowledge of what 'pairs' look like guides perception of the pattern
choices.explanation: Top-down processing involves higher-level knowledge influencing perception — the grouping of nearby elements into pairs is a bottom-up Gestalt process (proximity) driven by the sensory data itself, not by prior conceptual knowledge.

choices.text: Binocular disparity — the slight difference in each eye's retinal image creates depth perception between pairs
choices.explanation: Binocular disparity is a depth cue based on the slightly different images from each eye — it creates depth perception, not the perceptual grouping of flat elements into pairs.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: A student who just learned about confirmation bias immediately begins noticing examples of it in news media, social media, and everyday conversations. Before taking the psychology course, she had never noticed these patterns despite encountering them daily.

questions.question: Which perceptual concept best explains the sudden increase in noticing confirmation bias examples?

choices.text: Change blindness — the student previously failed to notice the bias because her attention was directed elsewhere
choices.explanation: Change blindness is the failure to detect changes when attention is directed elsewhere during the change — the student's increased noticing reflects a perceptual set created by new knowledge, not change blindness.

choices.text: Perceptual set — having a concept in mind makes the student more likely to notice and interpret stimuli through that lens
choices.explanation: Perceptual set is the tendency for expectations, context, and prior knowledge to influence perception — learning about confirmation bias created a schema that makes her more alert to and likely to recognize those patterns in everyday experience.

choices.text: The cocktail party effect — the student's brain now selectively filters for confirmation bias like her name being called in a crowd
choices.explanation: The cocktail party effect is selective attention to personally relevant signals (like your name) in a noisy environment — the student's pattern reflects perceptual set from newly acquired knowledge, not the cocktail party effect.

choices.text: Sensory adaptation — her sensory neurons have become more sensitive to signals related to cognitive bias
choices.explanation: Sensory adaptation involves reduced sensitivity with sustained stimulation at the sensory receptor level — increased noticing of a newly learned concept is a top-down perceptual set effect, not a change in sensory receptor sensitivity.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: A child who has learned the rule 'add -ed to verbs to make them past tense' begins saying 'goed' and 'runned' after previously using 'went' and 'ran' correctly. This error emerges even though adults consistently use correct irregular past tenses around the child.

questions.question: Which language development process does this error pattern best illustrate?

choices.text: Language acquisition device failure — the inborn grammar mechanism is malfunctioning, causing regression to earlier speech patterns
choices.explanation: The language acquisition device (Chomsky) is a theoretical inborn mechanism for grammar learning — overgeneralization is a normal, predictable stage of language acquisition, not a malfunction of the LAD.

choices.text: Overgeneralization — the child incorrectly applies a grammatical rule to irregular verbs that are exceptions to the rule
choices.explanation: Overgeneralization (overgeneralization errors) occurs when children learn a grammatical rule and apply it too broadly, including to irregular forms that are exceptions — saying 'goed' shows the child has learned the rule but not yet the irregular exceptions.

choices.text: Linguistic relativity — the child's native language structure is preventing correct encoding of irregular past tenses
choices.explanation: Linguistic relativity (Sapir-Whorf hypothesis) proposes that language influences thought across different languages — overgeneralization within a single language is a developmental learning error, not a cross-linguistic relativity effect.

choices.text: Telegraphic speech — the child is reducing sentences to essential words only, creating minimal utterances
choices.explanation: Telegraphic speech (2-word stage) involves dropping grammatical function words and keeping content words — the error described involves applying a grammar rule to irregular verbs, not simplification to telegraphic form.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: A rat in a Skinner box is trained to press a lever for food. After training is complete, the food dispenser is disabled and pressing the lever no longer delivers food. After 40 lever presses with no food reward, the rat stops pressing.

questions.question: Which operant conditioning process does the rat's cessation of lever pressing demonstrate?

choices.text: Extinction — the operant response declines when it is no longer followed by reinforcement
choices.explanation: Extinction in operant conditioning occurs when a previously reinforced behavior is no longer reinforced, causing the response rate to decrease and eventually cease — the rat's lever pressing extinguishes without the food reward.

choices.text: Negative punishment — the food has been removed, which punishes lever pressing and reduces its frequency
choices.explanation: Negative punishment involves removing a desired stimulus to decrease a behavior — extinction occurs when reinforcement simply stops being available, not when it is actively removed as a consequence of the behavior.

choices.text: Latent inhibition — the rat learned to ignore the lever because it repeatedly failed to produce food before training
choices.explanation: Latent inhibition refers to reduced conditioning to a stimulus that was previously presented without consequence — the rat was successfully trained (lever pressing was reinforced), so the cessation without reinforcement is extinction, not latent inhibition.

choices.text: Spontaneous recovery — the rat's behavior spontaneously returns to baseline after a rest period
choices.explanation: Spontaneous recovery is the reappearance of an extinguished response after a rest period — the rat's cessation of pressing describes extinction, not the later return of the response.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: A person entering a dimly lit restaurant initially struggles to read the menu. After a few minutes, her vision adjusts and she can read the menu easily even though the lighting level has not changed.

questions.question: Which sensory process explains the improved vision in the dark restaurant?

choices.text: Dark adaptation — photoreceptors (especially rods) adjust to the reduced light level, increasing sensitivity
choices.explanation: Dark adaptation is the process by which rods gradually increase in sensitivity as rhodopsin regenerates and the visual system adjusts to low-light conditions — it takes several minutes to complete, explaining improved menu reading after time in the dimly lit room.

choices.text: Absolute threshold lowering — the brain permanently reduces its detection threshold in all sensory systems after exposure to darkness
choices.explanation: The absolute threshold is not permanently altered by exposure to darkness — dark adaptation is a temporary, reversible change in photoreceptor sensitivity specific to the visual system, not a permanent cross-sensory threshold change.

choices.text: Top-down processing — knowing what the menu says allows the brain to fill in missing visual information
choices.explanation: Top-down processing uses prior knowledge to interpret ambiguous sensory input — the scenario describes a genuine improvement in visual sensitivity from photoreceptor adjustment (dark adaptation), not knowledge-based interpretation.

choices.text: Sensory adaptation — the nervous system reduces sensory input in response to sustained darkness, which paradoxically improves perception
choices.explanation: Sensory adaptation typically reduces sensitivity with sustained stimulation — dark adaptation specifically involves increasing photoreceptor sensitivity as the visual system adjusts to low light, which is the opposite of sensory adaptation reduction.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: In a study of observational learning, children aged 3-6 watched an adult either aggressively beat a Bobo doll or play quietly with other toys. Children who watched the aggressive model were significantly more likely to beat the Bobo doll themselves. Children who watched the non-aggressive model showed little aggression toward the doll.
